Document [1] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Etiology) Systemic lupus erythematosus and autoimmune disease: Osteonecrosis in patients with lupus is often attributed to chronic corticosteroid therapy; however, systemic lupus erythematosus and antiphospholipid antibodies may contribute to a hypercoagulable state. Young patients with lupus on steroids are at particularly high risk of multifocal osteonecrosis.
Document [2] (Title: Avascular Necrosis -- Etiology) Glucocorticoids used at high doses and for prolonged periods can induce osteonecrosis via osteocyte apoptosis. Apoptosis disrupts the lacunar-canicular system. [2] Other risk factors for AVN are alcohol misuse, blood dyscrasias, autoimmune diseases such as lupus, etc. The cause of interrupted blood flow is unknown for twenty-five percent of people with AVN. Document [3] (Title: Femoral Head Avascular Necrosis -- Etiology) Sickle cell disease can often precipitate osteonecrosis. The misshapen and rigid red blood cells impede blood flow leading to ischemia and bony infarction, with the femoral head being the most common site of osteonecrosis in these patients. [8] Autoimmune and chronic inflammatory disorders, e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), are well-known to be associated with osteonecrosis of the femoral head. The risk of developing the condition in these patients is usually attributed to long-term steroid treatment, though there are reports of cases in those who are steroid naive. [9]
Document [4] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Etiology) The unifying mechanism in these diverse conditions is the impairment of bone blood flow, resulting in the cellular death of bone marrow and osteocytes. The loss of viable bone triggers reparative processes that cannot keep pace with bone resorption, ultimately leading to structural weakness and collapse of the subchondral bone. No inherited genetic predisposition to SON has been identified; rather, 1 or more acquired risk factors underlie most cases. In many patients, multiple risk factors (eg, a patient with lupus on steroids and immunosuppressants) act synergistically to precipitate osteonecrosis. [7] [8]
Document [5] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Treatment / Management -- Nonoperative Management) Lifestyle modifications: Addressing risk factors contributing to osteonecrosis development is crucial. Patients are advised to stop smoking, avoid alcohol, maintain a healthy weight to reduce knee load, and control lipid levels if elevated. If SON is steroid-induced and the clinical situation allows, reducing the dose or using alternate therapies, with the prescribing clinician's guidance, can potentially slow further osteonecrosis. Optimizing control of underlying diseases, such as lupus activity, is also crucial in minimizing the need for high-risk medications.
Document [6] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies) Although no specific laboratory tests exist for diagnosing osteonecrosis, laboratory work is valuable for identifying potential underlying causes. Patients without a known diagnosis should be evaluated for conditions such as coagulopathies (eg, antiphospholipid antibodies, protein C/S deficiency), inflammatory diseases (eg, antinuclear antibody for lupus), or metabolic issues if clinical suspicion arises. In known cases (eg, lupus on steroids), labs directly guide the management of the underlying disease, but not the osteonecrosis itself. Joint infection or inflammatory arthritis should be ruled out if signs like significant warmth, redness, or very high inflammatory markers are present. SON typically exhibits only mild elevation of inflammatory markers, if any. [19]
Document [7] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Pearls and Other Issues -- General overview) SON occurs due to compromised blood supply, leading to subchondral bone death and eventual joint collapse. Unlike SONK, SON typically affects younger patients (<50 years old) and often involves multiple joints. Common risk factors include chronic corticosteroid use, alcohol abuse, sickle cell disease, autoimmune disorders such as lupus, and organ transplantation. MRI is the gold standard for early diagnosis, as plain radiographs may be normal in the early stages. Early recognition and intervention are key in SON. Nonoperative management may delay progression; however, joint-preserving surgery is preferred in early disease, while arthroplasty is typically required for advanced cases.
Document [8] (Title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Treatment / Management) Other management considerations: Hydroxychloroquine shall be used in all patients with SLE given its benefits beyond just managing active manifestations, including anti-thrombotic properties, and preventing flares. Patients on hydroxychloroquine will require regular ophthalmology exams to monitor for the rare but irreversible maculopathy associated with this drug. Corticosteroids are frequently used in SLE, with many patients unable to taper them completely. Long-term adverse effects of corticosteroids shall be considered and monitored, including osteoporosis, glaucoma, cataract, and avascular necrosis. Patients on high-dose corticosteroids will also need antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent infections. Most immunosuppressive agents used in SLE have several potential adverse effects ranging from cytopenias and hepatotoxicity with most to an increased risk of urinary bladder cancer with cyclophosphamide. These patients shall be appropriately and closely monitored for adverse effects of these agents.
Document [9] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Deterrence and Patient Education) Identify and modify risk factors: Any modifiable contributors such as tapering steroids to the lowest effective dose, recommending cessation of alcohol, and treating hyperlipidemia, should be addressed to reduce further osteonecrotic damage. Collaboration with the patient’s other clinician's such as rheumatologists for lupus or transplant clinicians is important.
Document [10] (Title: Avascular Necrosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education) Patient presentation of this disease is highly variable, so it is essential to reduce your risk factors. Ways to decrease risks include avoiding excessive alcohol consumption, no tobacco use, and tapering corticosteroids to the lowest necessary dose. Other lifestyle modifications like eating a healthy diet and maintaining an appropriate weight minimize joint damage. For manual laborers, consider avoiding repetitive activities that place strain on their joints. If possible, laborers should take regular breaks. Lastly, be proactive. Since patients with early-stage often do not have symptoms, they should seek periodic well-being evaluations by their primary care clinician. [1]

Document [12] (Title: Sickle Cell Crisis -- History and Physical -- Osteonecrosis) Bone, as an organ, can undergo infarction in sickle cell disease, leading to osteonecrosis. Osteonecrosis affects upwards of 50% of sickle patients [16] . Plain X-rays, though helpful, aren't the gold standard, as is the MRI. The MRI is superior in sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy. [17] Osteonecrosis in sickle cell disease appears primarily in the long bones and secondarily in sites such as the cranial sections. Osteonecrosis can be found in other ailments, too, including steroid use, HIV, SLE, renal failure, multiple sclerosis, Sjogren syndrome, COVID, SARS (Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome), leukemias, and lymphomas. The skeletal vaso-occlusive disease leads to inflammation with cytokine release, increased receptor activation of nuclear factor Kappa-Beta Ligand (RANK) signaling, and osteoclast activation. [18] Osteonecrosis of the hip brings infirmity (as well as depression, anxiety, and a decreased quality of life - QOL) by causing subchondral bone collapse, leading to pain and disability. [19] Vitamin D deficiency also occurs in about a third of patients and compounds the situation. Protective factors for hip pathology were Hyrdrea use and high fetal hemoglobin. Some risk factors included an Hgb / Hct ratio more than 0.33 and the presence of BMP 6 (bone morphogenic protein) genotypes AT-rs267196 and AG-rs267201. [20] The latter is associated with chondroblasts and osteoblasts, valuable in bone formation. The treatment approach is multifaceted. Osteoblast (ABMDO) transplantation is being sought as a means to regenerate bone and promote healing. [21] Intravenous bisphosphonates have been found to induce bone resorption as well as improve or resolve pain. [18] As Pyrophonate analogs, osteoclasts take up bisphosphonates, thereby causing their apoptosis. Interactions can occur in any non-long bone location, such as the vertebrae, which gives the endplate an H-shaped deformity, and in the cranium. Cranial bone infarctions may involve the mandibular bone and cause facial nerve palsy; orbital infarcts may result in subgaleal hematomas. [18] [22] [23] The MRI can display bone marrow edema and subperiosteal fluid. Bone infarcts can initiate a secondary effect of myositis on nearby muscles. [18] Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) becomes elevated and can be used as a marker for this finding. Parallel sequelae (eg, acute fasciitis, necrotizing myositis, and compartmental syndrome) can be seen.

It is also very resistant to treatments compared to other forms of cutaneous sarcoidosis. Document [15] (Title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- History and Physical -- Musculoskeletal Manifestations) Approximately 80 to 90% of patients with SLE suffer from musculoskeletal involvement at some point during their disease course and may range from mild arthralgias to deforming arthritis. Lupus arthritis is typically a non-erosive, symmetrical inflammatory polyarthritis affecting predominantly the small joints of the hands, knees, and wrists, although any joint can be involved. Jaccoud arthropathy results from the joint capsule and ligament laxity, leading to non-erosive deformities of the hands, including ulnar deviation and subluxation of the metacarpophalangeal joints that may mimic rheumatoid arthritis. Usually, these deformities are reducible, although rarely, they may become fixed. Avascular necrosis (with or without steroid use) can occur in up to 10% of patients with SLE and is usually bilateral and involves the hip joints. Inflammatory myopathy with histopathological features similar to but less striking than polymyositis has been seen in less than 10% of SLE cases. Patients with SLE are at high risk for the development of fibromyalgia, with incidences as high as 20% reported. Rheumatoid nodules have been reported in patients with SLE. [13]
Document [16] (Title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Complications) Medication-induced complications are common and require close monitoring. Long-term corticosteroid use in SLE patients frequently leads to under-diagnosed and under-treated osteoporosis, leading to osteoporotic fractures. Other complications of long-term use of corticosteroid therapy include avascular necrosis, glaucoma, cataract, weight gain, and poor control of Diabetes mellitus. High-dose corticosteroid use can also be associated with opportunistic infections and acute psychosis. Long-term use of hydroxychloroquine may rarely result in maculopathy and retinopathy that is irreversible, and close ophthalmology examinations are recommended. Cyclophosphamide use is associated with a significantly high risk of interstitial cystitis and bladder cancer even after drug discontinuation. SLE patients are immunocompromised and at a significantly high risk of infections which is one of the significant causes of morbidity and mortality in SLE. [15]
Document [17] (Title: Lupus Nephritis -- Pathophysiology) Results from multiple lupus nephritis studies indicate that class IV is the most common form and is associated with the worst prognosis. [29] [31] A meta-analysis revealed that 15% to 30% of patients with class IV disease fail to achieve remission, and of those who do, 15% to 30% experience relapse. [29] Additionally, patients with lupus nephritis face a higher risk of atherosclerosis, with coronary artery disease (CAD) being the leading cause of mortality in individuals who have had SLE for more than 5 years. The increased CAD risk is strongly linked to lupus nephritis, with underlying mechanisms including atherosclerosis, vasculitis, thrombosis, embolization, and vasospasm. The rate of fatal myocardial infarction in these patients is reported to be 3 times higher than in age-matched controls. [32]

Document [20] (Title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Introduction) Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a systemic autoimmune disease with multisystemic involvement. The condition has several phenotypes, with varying clinical presentations from mild mucocutaneous manifestations to multiorgan and severe central nervous system involvement. Several immunopathogenic pathways play a role in the development of SLE. Hargraves described the lupus erythematosus (LE cell) in 1948. Several pathogenic autoantibodies have since been identified. Despite recent advances in technology and understanding of the pathological basis and risk factors for SLE, the exact pathogenesis is still not well known. Diagnosis of SLE can be challenging, and while several classification criteria have been posed, their utility in the clinical setting is still a matter of debate. Management of SLE is dictated by organ system involvement. Despite several agents shown to be efficacious in treating SLE, the disease still poses significant morbidity and mortality risk in patients. [1]

Document [24] (Title: Femoral Head Avascular Necrosis -- Evaluation) A laboratory workup should be done to help rule out other causes of hip pain as well as to assess for comorbid factors in patients with suspected osteonecrosis. A workup may include a complete blood count (CBC), lipid panel, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), rheumatoid factor (RF), anti-nuclear antibody (ANA), anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP), and hemoglobin electrophoresis. Elevated ANA and/or RF would indicate an active autoimmune process but are non-specific. Both ESR and CRP are elevated by inflammatory processes but are also non-specific. Elevated anti-CCP antibodies are specific for rheumatoid arthritis, while hemoglobin electrophoresis showing HbS with a low concentration of HbF would indicate sickle cell disease. [19] [20] A CBC showing evidence of normocytic or microcytic anemia with an elevated reticulocyte count would also be consistent with a diagnosis of sickle cell disease. Rheumatoid arthritis and sickle cell disease are two conditions that can precipitate the development of osteonecrosis in the femoral head and can cause hip pain even without osteonecrosis.
Document [25] (Title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Continuing Education Activity) Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a systemic autoimmune disease with multisystem involvement and is associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Genetic, immunological, endocrine, and environmental factors influence the loss of immunological tolerance against self-antigens leading to the formation of pathogenic autoantibodies that cause tissue damage through multiple mechanisms. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of systemic lupus erythematosus and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

Document [29] (Title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Deterrence and Patient Education) While prognosis has improved due to advances in diagnostics and surgical techniques, SON remains a challenging condition to treat and requires long-term management. Emerging stem cell therapies and biomaterial-based approaches may further expand joint-preserving options. Throughout the management course, an interdisciplinary team approach is beneficial. This team typically consists of orthopedic surgeons, radiologists, physical therapists, rheumatologists, and other specialists to optimize local knee treatment and the patient's systemic health. The standard of care extends beyond treating the knee alone, focusing on optimizing the patient's overall health and condition; for example, managing lupus flare or switching to steroid-sparing medications to prevent new osteonecrotic lesions. Regular follow-up and imaging are necessary to monitor the lesion’s progression or healing status.

Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 94) Human corticotropin and multiple methylprednisolone formulations (acetate, aceponate, sodium phosphate, sodium succinate) are indicated for multiple sclerosis and a broad spectrum of blood disorders (اضطراب الدم), which encompass acute post‑hemorrhagic anemia, deficiency‑related anemia, globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم) and other disease‑injury entities; risk factors and the diagnostic test PBI are associated with these hematologic conditions, emphasizing comprehensive risk evaluation. Systemic lupus erythematosus/connective tissue disorders (SLE/CTD) are classified under vascular collagen disease (داء الكولاجين‑الوعائي) and frequently present with lupus nephritis (التهاب الكلية الذئبي), anemia, and the symptom feeling of weakness (الشعور بالضعف). SLE/CTD is treated with methylprednisolone variants, hydroxychloroquine (hydroxychlorochin) and contraindicates trioxsalen and zlato. Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041) Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 449) Methoxsalen is a psoralen medication that is contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية) and in infants (رضيع) because of an elevated risk of adverse reactions in these groups. Although some reports suggest that methoxsalen may be used to treat certain fungal infections (الفطريات الفطرية), its potential benefits must be carefully weighed against these contraindications. Systemic lupus erythematosus is clinically associated with other circulatory disease, indicating that individuals with SLE frequently experience cardiovascular complications. Other circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة), reflecting its underlying link to disorders of the extracellular matrix and vascular structures. Recognizing these interconnections assists clinicians in avoiding inappropriate methoxsalen use in vulnerable populations while remaining alert to the broader circulatory risks inherent in connective‑tissue diseases such as SLE.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327) The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1797) Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders are classified as a subtype of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (vascular collagen disease) and are clinically associated with اختلال وظيفة الكلى (kidney dysfunction). A specific manifestation of this renal involvement is التهاب الكلية الذئبي (lupus nephritis), which represents the renal child condition of the broader disease entity. In terms of pharmacologic management, hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, whereas the agents trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with these disorders. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship, the renal complications, and the appropriate versus inappropriate medications is essential for comprehensive patient care.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179) Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177) Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1293) Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of the broader collagen‑vascular disease category (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي). Several immunomodulatory and anti‑inflammatory agents are considered therapeutic options for this condition. Prednisone, triamcinolonacetonid (triamcinolone acetonide), and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, reflecting their roles in suppressing autoimmune activity and reducing disease‑related inflammation. Conversely, trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, indicating that these agents should be avoided because of potential adverse effects or disease exacerbation. Understanding both the beneficial treatments and the contraindicated substances is essential for safe and effective management of patients with this collagen‑vascular disease.

Procedures and conditions related to osteoporosis extend beyond fracture fixation; hip‑head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are also members of the osteoporosis SMQ, illustrating how severe bone‑density loss can lead to joint replacement and recurrent fractures. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that clavicular and shoulder‑arm surgical repairs are precisely linked to shoulder anatomy, that femoral intracapsular fractures correspond to hip neck fractures and are described in multiple languages, and that the classification of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2180) Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is categorized as a subtype of the broader collagen vascular disease (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي). Hydroxychlorochin may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, while the agents trioxsalen and Zlato are contraindicated in patients with this condition, underscoring important therapeutic choices and disease classification for patient care.

A noted but likely erroneous link between acute and unspecified renal failure and lower‑limb agents (كلاء الكليون السفلي) is recognized as a data inconsistency. Neonatal research concepts captured include accelerated atrial heart (تَسَرُّعُ القَلْبِ الأُذَينِيّ المنتبذ), connective‑tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة), and lower‑limb agents (وكلاء الكليون السفلي), highlighting the relevance of vascular and connective‑tissue entities in neonatal investigations.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623) Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.
